title: Gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master gravitational PE and kinetic energy for Cambridge 9702. Clear explanations, worked examples and exam tips for P1 and P2.
learningObjectives: Derive and apply the formula for gravitational potential energy (ΔEp = mgΔh) near the Earth's surface and explain the conditions under which it holds
Use the kinetic energy formula (Ek = ½mv²) to calculate the kinetic energy of a moving object and relate changes in speed to changes in kinetic energy
Apply conservation of energy to solve problems involving interchange between gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy, accounting for energy dissipated by resistive forces

simpleExplanation.title: Energy swapping: height ↔ speed
simpleExplanation.summary: Gravitational potential energy (GPE) is the energy stored in an object because of its height above a reference level. As an object falls, GPE converts into kinetic energy (KE) — the energy of motion. In a frictionless world, the total mechanical energy stays constant; in reality, some energy is lost to heat and sound.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a rollercoaster: at the top of the first hill the cart is loaded with GPE. As it races downhill that GPE is 'spent' on speed — the cart gains KE. Any slight slowing compared with the ideal case is energy lost to friction in the wheels and air resistance.
steps: Learn both formulae cold: ΔEp = mgΔh and Ek = ½mv², and know every symbol and its SI unit.
Always define your reference level for height (usually the lowest point in the problem) so that Δh is unambiguous.
Set up an energy equation: Ep(initial) + Ek(initial) = Ep(final) + Ek(final) + energy dissipated, then solve for the unknown.
Check units at every step — mass in kg, g = 9.81 m s⁻², height in m gives energy in joules (J).

sections.content: Energy is the engine of every physics problem. In topic 5.2 you meet two forms that swap back and forth constantly: **gravitational potential energy (GPE)** — stored energy due to an object's position in a gravitational field — and **kinetic energy (KE)** — energy possessed by a moving object. Cambridge examiners love questions that require you to track how energy transfers between these two forms, so a solid command of the formulae and the principle of **conservation of energy** will earn you marks across both Paper 1 and Paper 2.

Near the Earth's surface the gravitational field strength g is approximately constant at 9.81 N kg⁻¹. When an object of mass m is raised through a vertical height Δh against gravity, work is done on the object and it gains gravitational potential energy. This change in GPE is given by the formula below. The reference level (Δh = 0) is chosen for convenience — it could be the floor, the ground, or any fixed horizontal plane — but you must be consistent throughout a calculation. Note that the formula only applies where g is constant, i.e. close to the Earth's surface; for large distances from Earth, a different expression involving the gravitational field at each point is needed (covered at A2).
ΔEp = mgΔh    where m = mass (kg), g = gravitational field strength (9.81 N kg⁻¹ at Earth's surface), Δh = change in vertical height (m), ΔEp = change in gravitational potential energy (J)

Any object with mass m moving at speed v possesses kinetic energy. The relationship is derived from the work–energy theorem: the net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy. Starting from rest and accelerating uniformly, W = Fs = mas, and using v² = u² + 2as gives the standard result. Kinetic energy is always positive (it depends on v², so direction does not matter) and is measured in joules.
Ek = ½mv²    where m = mass (kg), v = speed (m s⁻¹), Ek = kinetic energy (J)
Conservation of Energy and Energy Transfers

The **principle of conservation of energy** states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred from one form to another. For a system where only gravity does work (no friction, no air resistance), the total mechanical energy — GPE plus KE — is constant at every point. When resistive forces are present, some mechanical energy is converted to thermal energy (heat) in the surroundings. The energy equation then becomes: (GPE + KE) at start = (GPE + KE) at end + energy transferred to thermal store. Examiners frequently ask you to calculate this 'lost' energy or to use it to find an average resistive force (using W = Fd).

items: ΔEp = mgΔh only holds when g is uniform — always state this assumption if asked to justify the formula.
Kinetic energy depends on v², so doubling speed quadruples KE; halving mass halves KE — this ratio logic appears often in MCQ distractors.
Always choose a clear reference level for height and stick to it; many errors come from measuring height inconsistently.
Energy 'lost' to friction or air resistance equals the difference between expected mechanical energy and actual mechanical energy — it is not truly lost, just dissipated as heat.
To find a resistive force F from energy dissipated W over distance d, use F = W/d (assuming force is constant and parallel to displacement).
In P2, show every substitution explicitly and include units — Cambridge mark schemes award method marks for correct working even if the final answer is wrong.

sections.question: A skier of mass 72 kg starts from rest at the top of a slope and reaches the bottom, which is 45 m vertically below the starting point, with a speed of 24 m s⁻¹. (a) Calculate the decrease in gravitational potential energy of the skier. (b) Calculate the kinetic energy of the skier at the bottom of the slope. (c) Determine the energy dissipated by friction and air resistance during the descent. (d) The slope has a length of 180 m. Assuming the resistive force is constant, calculate its magnitude. (g = 9.81 m s⁻²)
sections.solution:
Part (a) — Decrease in GPE:
ΔEp = mgΔh
ΔEp = 72 × 9.81 × 45
ΔEp = 31 752 J ≈ 3.18 × 10⁴ J

Part (b) — Kinetic energy at the bottom:
Ek = ½mv²
Ek = ½ × 72 × 24²
Ek = ½ × 72 × 576
Ek = 20 736 J ≈ 2.07 × 10⁴ J

Part (c) — Energy dissipated:
Energy dissipated = ΔEp − Ek
Energy dissipated = 31 752 − 20 736
Energy dissipated = 11 016 J ≈ 1.10 × 10⁴ J

Part (d) — Resistive force:
W = Fd  →  F = W/d
F = 11 016 / 180
F ≈ 61.2 N ≈ 61 N (3 s.f.)

Key checks: GPE > KE (energy was lost to friction ✓); F is positive and much smaller than the component of gravity along the slope ✓.

sections.content: Cambridge P2 mark schemes almost always require you to state the energy equation before substituting values. Write 'loss in GPE = gain in KE + energy dissipated' (or equivalent) as a single line before any numbers — this earns a dedicated method mark and also keeps your own working organised. In MCQ (P1), eliminate options by checking whether the answer has the correct units and whether it changes in the right direction when a variable (e.g. mass or height) is doubled — this catches most common distractors without lengthy calculation.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: What is the difference between gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy in Cambridge 9702 Physics?
faq.a: Gravitational potential energy (GPE) is the energy an object has because of its height in a gravitational field, calculated using ΔEp = mgΔh. Kinetic energy (KE) is the energy an object has because it is moving, calculated using Ek = ½mv². In Cambridge 9702, questions on this topic usually involve converting one form into the other — for example a falling object losing GPE and gaining KE. You can practise this skill with real past-paper questions in our free course at /subjects/9702.

faq.q: How do I handle energy 'lost' to friction in 9702 energy questions?
faq.a: Energy dissipated by friction or air resistance equals the initial mechanical energy minus the final mechanical energy: dissipated energy = (GPE + KE)_initial − (GPE + KE)_final. This thermal energy is not destroyed but transferred to the surroundings. Cambridge examiners often then ask you to find the average resistive force using F = W/d, where d is the distance travelled along the path. Our worked examples and past-paper marking tool at /mark?subject=9702&topic=5.2 will help you practise writing this out correctly for full marks.

faq.q: Why does the formula ΔEp = mgΔh only work near the Earth's surface?
faq.a: The formula ΔEp = mgΔh assumes that the gravitational field strength g is constant throughout the motion. Near the Earth's surface this is an excellent approximation because the change in distance from the Earth's centre is negligible compared with the Earth's radius. At very large distances from Earth, g decreases significantly with height and you need a more advanced expression (covered in the A2 gravitational fields topic). In AS Level papers (9702 P1 and P2), you will always be in the 'constant g' regime, so the formula applies — but it is good exam technique to state this assumption if a question asks you to justify your working.
